title: Repository-native query-conditioned coarse-to-fine prompt compression
goal: Implement and evaluate a deterministic-trust-boundary prompt compiler that combines exact repository authority and invariant-hypergraph closure with question-aware coarse selection, explicit position scheduling, relevance-conditioned dynamic budgets, protected atom/span fine compression, tokenizer-specific rendering, and provenance-bounded recovery so coding-agent prompts reduce token cost without weakening execution or evidence fidelity.
constraints: Compressed prompts are derived projections; canonical authority and task capsules remain semantic owners
Mandatory invariant-hypergraph closure, task-local authority, and output/stop contracts are included before any ranking or pruning
Learned, perplexity, embedding, symbolic, and hypervector signals remain P0 ranking hypotheses and cannot establish authority or P2 correspondence
Compression operates first on typed atoms or recoverable spans; arbitrary subword deletion is not the initial implementation
Negation, branch rules, exact argv commands, paths, SHAs, evidence classes, and stop conditions are protected
Token cost is measured per target tokenizer, never inferred from characters
Recovery is deterministic, digest-bound, and rejects ambiguous mappings
No P0, P1, P2, or P3 result promotes itself to P4
required_methods: Question-aware coarse selection with deterministic structural baseline and optional p(task plus restrictive suffix given context) adapter
Reversed conditional direction and no-restrictive-suffix controls
Explicit document or atom position scheduler with dependency-order constraints and position-sweep evaluation
Dynamic relevance-ranked budget allocation with mandatory closure and risk floors
Optional contrastive task-conditioned atom importance compared with ordinary perplexity
Deterministic expansion and provenance-bounded output identifier recovery
Factorial ablations isolating each imported component
success: At least 50 percent median token reduction versus canonical prose on declared tokenizers
Zero critical invariant loss on adversarial benchmark cases
Exact command, path, SHA, negation, evidence-class, and stop-condition retention
Non-inferior execution behavior established within a predeclared margin on available model runs
At least one held-out task set demonstrates whether each coarse selector, position scheduler, dynamic budget, contrastive scorer, and recovery component has causal benefit
Compression and recovery overhead remains below saved inference cost for admitted defaults
Exact-head repository tests and build pass
falsifiers: Any branch, worktree-preservation, authority, negation, exact-command, path, SHA, stop-condition, or evidence-boundary invariant is lost
Question-aware coarse ranking fails to improve gold-node Recall@k over deterministic structural or lexical baselines
Contrastive atom scoring fails to outperform ordinary perplexity or deterministic atom selection at equal budgets
Dynamic budgets fail to outperform uniform budgets on heterogeneous-relevance tasks
Position scheduling does not reduce middle-position execution variance
Recovery corrupts or ambiguously rewrites identifiers, commands, paths, or SHAs
Symbolic rendering increases token count or execution variance without compensating benefit
Hypervector retrieval fails to outperform deterministic diffusion or introduces authority drift
Compression and decompression overhead exceeds saved inference cost
The mathematical layer does not alter selection, proof obligations, ablation design, or verification
research_inputs: Chung et al., Physical Review X 8, 031003 (2018)
